The default service account should be configured such that it does not provide a service account token and does not have any explicit rights assignments.
audit: For each namespace in the cluster, review the rights assigned to the default service account and ensure that it has no roles or cluster roles bound to it apart from the defaults. Additionally ensure that the automountServiceAccountToken: false setting is in place for each default service account.
remediation: Create explicit service accounts wherever a Kubernetes workload requires specific access to the Kubernetes API server. Modify the configuration of each default service account to include this value automountServiceAccountToken: false
impact: All workloads which require access to the Kubernetes API will require an explicit service account to be created.
default_value: By default the default service account allows for its service account token to be mounted in pods in its namespace.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the tasks required for this compliance control can be effectively automated using Kubernetes APIs and `kubectl` commands. The necessary checks, such as verifying role bindings and the `automountServiceAccountToken` setting, can be performed programmatically by querying Kubernetes resources. This aligns with Kubernetes capabilities, allowing for efficient and consistent compliance checks across all namespaces.
automation_summary: - **Kubernetes Resource/Service:** ServiceAccounts, RoleBindings, ClusterRoleBindings - **API/CLI Method:** - `kubectl get serviceaccounts --all-namespaces -o json` - `kubectl get rolebindings --all-namespaces -o json` - `kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o json` - **Properties to Check:** - Ensure default service accounts have no additional roles or bindings beyond defaults. - Verify `automountServiceAccountToken` is set to `false` for default service accounts. - **Pass Criteria:** - Default service accounts have no additional roles or bindings and `automountServiceAccountToken` is set to `false`. - **Fail Criteria:** - Any default service account with additional roles or bindings, or `automountServiceAccountToken` not set to `false`.

title: Ensure that Service Account Tokens are only mounted where necessary
description: Service accounts tokens should not be mounted in pods except where the workload running in the pod explicitly needs to communicate with the API server
rationale: Mounting service account tokens inside pods can provide an avenue for privilege escalation attacks where an attacker is able to compromise a single pod in the cluster. Avoiding mounting these tokens removes this attack avenue.
audit: Review pod and service account objects in the cluster and ensure that the option below is set, unless the resource explicitly requires this access. automountServiceAccountToken: false
remediation: Modify the definition of pods and service accounts which do not need to mount service account tokens to disable it.
impact: Pods mounted without service account tokens will not be able to communicate with the API server, except where the resource is available to unauthenticated principals.
default_value: By default, all pods get a service account token mounted in them.
justification: The compliance control can be effectively audited using Kubernetes APIs and CLI tools like `kubectl`. The task involves checking the `automountServiceAccountToken` setting for pods, which is accessible via the Kubernetes API. Both AI opinions agree on the feasibility of automation, though the second opinion suggests a more precise program name. The critical aspect of determining if a pod requires API access can be managed by additional logic or policy definitions, but the primary check is automatable.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o=jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.spec.serviceAccountName}{"\t"}{.spec.automountServiceAccountToken}{"\n"}{end}'` - Properties to Check: `.spec.automountServiceAccountToken` - Pass Criteria: `automountServiceAccountToken` is set to `false` for pods that do not require API access. - Fail Criteria: `automountServiceAccountToken` is set to `true` for pods that do not require API access.

title: Avoid use of system:masters group
description: The special group system:masters should not be used to grant permissions to any user or service account, except where strictly necessary (e.g. bootstrapping access prior to RBAC being fully available)
rationale: The system:masters group has unrestricted access to the Kubernetes API hard-coded into the API server source code. An authenticated user who is a member of this group cannot have their access reduced, even if all bindings and cluster role bindings which mention it, are removed. When combined with client certificate authentication, use of this group can allow for irrevocable cluster-admin level credentials to exist for a cluster.
audit: Review a list of all credentials which have access to the cluster and ensure that the group system:masters is not used.
remediation: Remove the system:masters group from all users in the cluster.
impact: Once the RBAC system is operational in a cluster system:masters should not be specifically required, as ordinary bindings from principals to the cluster-admin cluster role can be made where unrestricted access is required.
default_value: By default some clusters will create a "break glass" client certificate which is a member of this group. Access to this client certificate should be carefully controlled and it should not be used for general cluster operations.
justification: The task of verifying that the `system:masters` group is not used in RoleBindings and ClusterRoleBindings can be effectively automated using Kubernetes CLI commands. Both AI opinions agree that this check can be performed using `kubectl` commands to query the necessary resources. The use of `jq` for JSON parsing is a standard practice to filter and identify any bindings that reference the `system:masters` group, making automation feasible and efficient.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: RoleBindings, ClusterRoleBindings - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get clusterrolebindings`, `kubectl get rolebindings` - Properties to Check: `.subjects[].name` for the presence of `system:masters` - Pass Criteria: No RoleBindings or ClusterRoleBindings should reference the `system:masters` group. - Fail Criteria: Any RoleBinding or ClusterRoleBinding that includes a subject with the name `system:masters` indicates non-compliance.

title: Limit use of the Bind, Impersonate and Escalate permissions in the Kubernetes cluster
description: Cluster roles and roles with the impersonate, bind or escalate permissions should not be granted unless strictly required. Each of these permissions allow a particular subject to escalate their privileges beyond those explicitly granted by cluster administrators
rationale: The impersonate privilege allows a subject to impersonate other users gaining their rights to the cluster. The bind privilege allows the subject to add a binding to a cluster role or role which escalates their effective permissions in the cluster. The escalate privilege allows a subject to modify cluster roles to which they are bound, increasing their rights to that level. Each of these permissions has the potential to allow for privilege escalation to cluster-admin level.
audit: Review the users who have access to cluster roles or roles which provide the impersonate, bind or escalate privileges.
remediation: Where possible, remove the impersonate, bind and escalate rights from subjects.
impact: There are some cases where these permissions are required for cluster service operation, and care should be taken before removing these permissions from system service accounts.
default_value: In a default kubeadm cluster, the system:masters group and clusterrole-aggregation-controller service account have access to the escalate privilege. The system:masters group also has access to bind and impersonate.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the control can be effectively audited using Kubernetes APIs and `kubectl` commands. The permissions in question (impersonate, bind, escalate) are accessible through the Kubernetes RBAC API, allowing for automated checks of ClusterRoles and RoleBindings. This aligns with the control's requirement to review users with specific permissions, which can be efficiently automated.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: RBAC (ClusterRoles, Roles, ClusterRoleBindings, RoleBindings) - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get clusterroles -o json`, `kubectl get roles --all-namespaces -o json`, `kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o json`, `kubectl get rolebindings --all-namespaces -o json` - Properties to Check: `rules` section for `impersonate`, `bind`, and `escalate` permissions - Pass Criteria: No ClusterRoles or Roles should have the `impersonate`, `bind`, or `escalate` permissions unless explicitly required for operational purposes. - Fail Criteria: Presence of `impersonate`, `bind`, or `escalate` permissions in any ClusterRoles or Roles without explicit operational necessity.

title: Minimize access to create persistent volumes
description: The ability to create persistent volumes in a cluster can provide an opportunity for privilege escalation, via the creation of hostPath volumes. As persistent volumes are not covered by Pod Security Admission, a user with access to create persistent volumes may be able to get access to sensitive files from the underlying host even where restrictive Pod Security Admission policies are in place.
rationale: The ability to create persistent volumes in a cluster opens up possibilities for privilege escalation and should be restricted, where possible.
audit: Review the users who have create access to PersistentVolume objects in the Kubernetes API.
remediation: Where possible, remove create access to PersistentVolume objects in the cluster.
justification: The task of minimizing access to create persistent volumes can be effectively automated using Kubernetes APIs. Both AI opinions agree that querying RoleBindings and ClusterRoleBindings for permissions related to persistent volumes is feasible through automated means. The use of `kubectl` commands to retrieve and analyze these bindings ensures that the process can be consistently and accurately executed without manual intervention.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: RoleBindings, ClusterRoleBindings - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get rolebindings --all-namespaces -o json`, `kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o json` - Properties to Check: Permissions associated with the `create` verb on the `persistentvolumes` resource - Pass Criteria: No unauthorized users/groups have `create` access to `persistentvolumes` - Fail Criteria: Any unauthorized users/groups have `create` access to `persistentvolumes`

title: Minimize access to the proxy sub-resource of nodes
description: Users with access to the Proxy sub-resource of Node objects automatically have permissions to use the kubelet API, which may allow for privilege escalation or bypass cluster security controls such as audit logs. The kubelet provides an API which includes rights to execute commands in any container running on the node. Access to this API is covered by permissions to the main Kubernetes API via the node object. The proxy sub-resource specifically allows wide ranging access to the kubelet API. Direct access to the kubelet API bypasses controls like audit logging (there is no audit log of kubelet API access) and admission control.
rationale: The ability to use the proxy sub-resource of node objects opens up possibilities for privilege escalation and should be restricted, where possible.
audit: Review the users who have access to the proxy sub-resource of node objects in the Kubernetes API.
remediation: Where possible, remove access to the proxy sub-resource of node objects.
justification: The control involves reviewing access permissions to the proxy sub-resource of node objects, which can be effectively audited using Kubernetes APIs and kubectl commands. Both AI opinions agree that the majority of this task can be automated by querying RoleBindings and ClusterRoleBindings to identify permissions related to the `nodes/proxy` resource. The use of Kubernetes APIs allows for precise and repeatable checks without the need for manual inspection.
automation_summary: - **Kubernetes Resource/Service:** RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) - **API/CLI Method:** Use `kubectl` to list and describe RoleBindings and ClusterRoleBindings. - **Properties to Check:** Check for rules in Role and ClusterRole definitions that include verbs like `get`, `list`, or `proxy` on the `nodes/proxy` resource. - **Pass Criteria:** No Role or ClusterRole grants access to `nodes/proxy` for any users or service accounts. - **Fail Criteria:** Any Role or ClusterRole grants access to `nodes/proxy` with the verbs `get`, `list`, or `proxy`.

title: Minimize access to the approval sub-resource of certificatesigningrequests objects
description: Users with access to the update the approval sub-resource of CertificateSigningRequests objects can approve new client certificates for the Kubernetes API effectively allowing them to create new high-privileged user accounts. This can allow for privilege escalation to full cluster administrator, depending on users configured in the cluster
rationale: The ability to update certificate signing requests should be limited.
audit: Review the users who have access to update the approval sub-resource of CertificateSigningRequests objects in the Kubernetes API.
remediation: Where possible, remove access to the approval sub-resource of CertificateSigningRequests objects.
justification: The task of reviewing users who have access to update the approval sub-resource of CertificateSigningRequests objects can be effectively automated using Kubernetes APIs and `kubectl` commands. Both AI opinions agree on the feasibility of automation, and the necessary data can be retrieved and analyzed programmatically. The task involves checking RoleBindings and ClusterRoleBindings, which is well-suited for automation.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: RoleBindings, ClusterRoleBindings - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get rolebindings --all-namespaces -o json`, `kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o json` - Properties to Check: Permissions related to `certificates.k8s.io` with `verbs: ["update"]` and `subresource: ["approval"]` on `certificatesigningrequests` - Pass Criteria: No Role or ClusterRole grants the `update` permission on the `approval` sub-resource of `certificatesigningrequests`. - Fail Criteria: At least one Role or ClusterRole grants the `update` permission on the `approval` sub-resource of `certificatesigningrequests`.

title: Minimize access to webhook configuration objects
description: Users with rights to create/modify/delete validatingwebhookconfigurations or mutatingwebhookconfigurations can control webhooks that can read any object admitted to the cluster, and in the case of mutating webhooks, also mutate admitted objects. This could allow for privilege escalation or disruption of the operation of the cluster.
rationale: The ability to manage webhook configuration should be limited
audit: Review the users who have access to validatingwebhookconfigurations or mutatingwebhookconfigurations objects in the Kubernetes API.
remediation: Where possible, remove access to the validatingwebhookconfigurations or mutatingwebhookconfigurations objects
justification: The control requires reviewing access to `validatingwebhookconfigurations` and `mutatingwebhookconfigurations`, which can be effectively done using Kubernetes APIs and CLI commands. Both AI opinions agree that this can be automated, and the necessary information can be retrieved using `kubectl` commands to query RBAC roles and bindings. The Kubernetes CLI provides sufficient capability to automate this compliance check.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: RBAC (Roles, ClusterRoles, RoleBindings, ClusterRoleBindings) - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get clusterroles`, `kubectl get roles`, `kubectl get clusterrolebindings`, `kubectl get rolebindings` - Properties to Check: `.rules[].resources` for roles and `.roleRef.name` for bindings - Pass Criteria: No roles or bindings grant access to `validatingwebhookconfigurations` or `mutatingwebhookconfigurations` to unauthorized users - Fail Criteria: Any role or binding grants access to `validatingwebhookconfigurations` or `mutatingwebhookconfigurations` to unauthorized users

title: Minimize access to the service account token creation
description: Users with rights to create new service account tokens at a cluster level, can create long-lived privileged credentials in the cluster. This could allow for privilege escalation and persistent access to the cluster, even if the users account has been revoked.
rationale: The ability to create service account tokens should be limited.
audit: Review the users who have access to create the token sub-resource of serviceaccount objects in the Kubernetes API.
remediation: Where possible, remove access to the token sub-resource of serviceaccount objects.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the audit can be automated using Kubernetes APIs and `kubectl` commands to query RBAC permissions. The ability to check for roles that allow the creation of service account tokens is feasible through these methods, making manual inspection unnecessary. The command refinement suggested by the second AI opinion enhances the accuracy of the automation.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get clusterroles` and `kubectl get clusterrolebindings` - Properties to Check: Roles and bindings that include the verb `create` for the resource `serviceaccounts/token` - Pass Criteria: No `ClusterRole` or `Role` should allow `create` on `serviceaccounts/token`. - Fail Criteria: Any `ClusterRole` or `Role` found that allows `create` on `serviceaccounts/token` indicates non-compliance.

title: Ensure that the cluster has at least one active policy control mechanism in place
description: Every Kubernetes cluster should have at least one policy control mechanism in place to enforce the other requirements in this section. This could be the in-built Pod Security Admission controller, or a third party policy control system.
rationale: Without an active policy control mechanism, it is not possible to limit the use of containers with access to underlying cluster nodes, via mechanisms like privileged containers, or the use of hostPath volume mounts.
audit: Pod Security Admission is enabled by default on all clusters using Kubernetes 1.23 or higher. To assess what controls, if any, are in place using this mechanism, review the namespaces in the cluster to see if the required labels have been applied kubectl get namespaces -o yaml To confirm if any external policy control system is in use, review the cluster for the presence of validatingadmissionwebhook and mutatingadmissionwebhook objects. kubectl get validatingwebhookconfigurations kubectl get mutatingwebhookconfigurations
remediation: Ensure that either Pod Security Admission or an external policy control system is in place for every namespace which contains user workloads.
impact: Where policy control systems are in place, there is a risk that workloads required for the operation of the cluster may be stopped from running. Care is required when implementing admission control policies to ensure that this does not occur.
default_value: By default, Pod Security Admission is enabled but no policies are in place.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the compliance control can be verified using Kubernetes API capabilities through `kubectl` commands. The commands provided allow for the automated checking of Pod Security Admission and the presence of validating and mutating admission webhooks, which are essential for policy control mechanisms. The Kubernetes CLI provides sufficient functionality to automate these checks without requiring manual intervention.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Namespaces, ValidatingWebhookConfigurations, MutatingWebhookConfigurations - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get namespaces -o yaml`, `kubectl get validatingwebhookconfigurations`, `kubectl get mutatingwebhookconfigurations` - Properties to Check: - For Namespaces: Check for labels `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce`, `pod-security.kubernetes.io/audit`, `pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn` - For Webhooks: Presence of any configurations in `validatingwebhookconfigurations` and `mutatingwebhookconfigurations` - Pass Criteria: - At least one of the required labels is present in the namespaces. - At least one validating and one mutating webhook configuration is present. - Fail Criteria: - None of the required labels are present in any namespace. - No validating or mutating webhook configurations are found.

title: Minimize the admission of privileged containers
description: Do not generally permit containers to be run with the securityContext.privileged flag set to true .
rationale: Privileged containers have access to all Linux Kernel capabilities and devices. A container running with full privileges can do almost everything that the host can do. This flag exists to allow special use-cases, like manipulating the network stack and accessing devices. There should be at least one admission control policy defined which does not permit privileged containers. If you need to run privileged containers, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that each policy disallows the admission of privileged containers.
remediation: Add policies to each namespace in the cluster which has user workloads to restrict the admission of privileged containers.
impact: Pods defined with spec.containers[].securityContext.privileged: true , spec.initContainers[].securityContext.privileged: true and spec.ephemeralContainers[].securityContext.privileged: true will not be permitted.
default_value: By default, there are no restrictions on the creation of privileged containers.
justification: The control can be effectively automated using Kubernetes APIs and CLI tools to verify the presence of policies that restrict privileged containers. Although Pod Security Policies (PSPs) are deprecated, alternative mechanisms such as Open Policy Agent (OPA) with Gatekeeper or Pod Security Admission (PSA) can be used to enforce and verify compliance. Both AI opinions agree on the feasibility of automation, with the second opinion highlighting the need to adapt to newer methods.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pod Security Admission (PSA) or Open Policy Agent (OPA) with Gatekeeper - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get constraints` for OPA/Gatekeeper or `kubectl get pods --namespace=<namespace> --field-selector=spec.securityContext.privileged=true` for PSA - Properties to Check: Ensure policies or constraints do not allow `spec.securityContext.privileged=true` - Pass Criteria: No policies or constraints allow privileged containers - Fail Criteria: Any policy or constraint allows `spec.securityContext.privileged=true`

title: Minimize the admission of containers wishing to share the host process ID namespace
description: Do not generally permit containers to be run with the hostPID flag set to true.
rationale: A container running in the host's PID namespace can inspect processes running outside the container. If the container also has access to ptrace capabilities this can be used to escalate privileges outside of the container. There should be at least one admission control policy defined which does not permit containers to share the host PID namespace. If you need to run containers which require hostPID, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that each policy disallows the admission of hostPID containers
remediation: Add policies to each namespace in the cluster which has user workloads to restrict the admission of hostPID containers.
impact: Pods defined with spec.hostPID: true will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
default_value: By default, there are no restrictions on the creation of hostPID containers.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the compliance control can be automated using Kubernetes APIs and kubectl commands. The task involves verifying the presence of policies that restrict the use of the `hostPID` flag, which can be efficiently checked through Kubernetes API calls. Although Pod Security Policies (PSPs) are deprecated, the use of Pod Security Admission (PSA) controls or custom admission controllers can be programmatically verified, making automation feasible.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Namespaces, Pod Security Admission - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get ns --show-labels`, `kubectl describe <psa/policy>` - Properties to Check: Labels indicating Pod Security Standards, specific policy settings for `hostPID` - Pass Criteria: No policies allow `spec.hostPID: true` for any pods in user namespaces. - Fail Criteria: At least one policy allows `spec.hostPID: true` or there are no policies restricting it.

title: Minimize the admission of containers wishing to share the host IPC namespace
description: Do not generally permit containers to be run with the hostIPC flag set to true.
rationale: A container running in the host's IPC namespace can use IPC to interact with processes outside the container. There should be at least one admission control policy defined which does not permit containers to share the host IPC namespace. If you need to run containers which require hostIPC, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that each policy disallows the admission of hostIPC containers
remediation: Add policies to each namespace in the cluster which has user workloads to restrict the admission of hostIPC containers.
impact: Pods defined with spec.hostIPC: true will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
default_value: By default, there are no restrictions on the creation of hostIPC containers.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the audit can be automated using Kubernetes APIs and CLI commands. The control involves verifying admission policies and pod specifications, which can be efficiently checked using Kubernetes API calls and `kubectl` commands. The ability to query and validate the `spec.hostIPC` field in pod specifications and check for admission policies aligns with the capabilities of Kubernetes APIs, making automation feasible.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods, Namespaces - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o jsonpath='{.items[?(@.spec.hostIPC==true)]}'`, `kubectl get ns --show-labels` - Properties to Check: `spec.hostIPC` field in pod specifications, namespace labels indicating security policies - Pass Criteria: No pods in any namespace have `spec.hostIPC: true` unless explicitly allowed by a defined policy. - Fail Criteria: Any pod with `spec.hostIPC: true` is found without an appropriate admission policy restricting it.

title: Minimize the admission of containers wishing to share the host network namespace
description: Do not generally permit containers to be run with the hostNetwork flag set to true.
rationale: A container running in the host's network namespace could access the local loopback device, and could access network traffic to and from other pods. There should be at least one admission control policy defined which does not permit containers to share the host network namespace. If you need to run containers which require access to the host's network namespaces, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that each policy disallows the admission of hostNetwork containers
remediation: Add policies to each namespace in the cluster which has user workloads to restrict the admission of hostNetwork containers.
impact: Pods defined with spec.hostNetwork: true will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
default_value: By default, there are no restrictions on the creation of hostNetwork containers.
justification: The compliance control can be effectively audited using Kubernetes APIs and CLI commands. Both AI opinions agree on the feasibility of using automated methods to check the configurations, although the second opinion suggests additional manual verification. However, the primary compliance check—ensuring that policies disallow `hostNetwork`—can be automated by inspecting PodSecurityAdmission configurations and namespace labels. The deprecation of PodSecurityPolicies is noted, but the automation can focus on PodSecurityAdmission, which is supported in newer Kubernetes versions.
automation_summary: - **Kubernetes Resource/Service:** PodSecurityAdmission, Namespaces - **API/CLI Method:** - `kubectl get ns --show-labels` - `kubectl get podsecurityadmission` - **Properties to Check:** - Namespace labels for Pod Security Standards compliance - PodSecurityAdmission configurations to ensure `hostNetwork` is disallowed - **Pass Criteria:** - All namespaces have labels indicating compliance with Pod Security Standards, specifically disallowing `hostNetwork`. - PodSecurityAdmission configurations do not permit `hostNetwork`. - **Fail Criteria:** - Any namespace or PodSecurityAdmission configuration that allows `hostNetwork` without restrictions.

title: Minimize the admission of containers with allowPrivilegeEscalation
description: Do not generally permit containers to be run with the allowPrivilegeEscalation flag set to true. Allowing this right can lead to a process running a container getting more rights than it started with. It's important to note that these rights are still constrained by the overall container sandbox, and this setting does not relate to the use of privileged containers.
rationale: A container running with the allowPrivilegeEscalation flag set to true may have processes that can gain more privileges than their parent. There should be at least one admission control policy defined which does not permit containers to allow privilege escalation. The option exists (and is defaulted to true) to permit setuid binaries to run. If you have need to run containers which use setuid binaries or require privilege escalation, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that each policy disallows the admission of containers which allow privilege escalation.
remediation: Add policies to each namespace in the cluster which has user workloads to restrict the admission of containers with .spec.allowPrivilegeEscalation set to true .
impact: Pods defined with spec.allowPrivilegeEscalation: true will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
default_value: By default, there are no restrictions on contained process ability to escalate privileges, within the context of the container.
justification: The compliance control can be effectively audited using Kubernetes APIs and CLI commands. Both AI opinions agree on the feasibility of automation, although the second opinion correctly points out the need to use Pod Security Admission (PSA) or Pod Security Standards (PSS) instead of the deprecated Pod Security Policies (PSP). Kubernetes 1.23 and later versions support these mechanisms, making it possible to automate the check for `allowPrivilegeEscalation` settings.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods - API/CLI Method: Use `kubectl` to interact with the Kubernetes API, specifically focusing on Pod Security Admission (PSA) or Pod Security Standards (PSS). - Properties to Check: `.spec.containers[*].securityContext.allowPrivilegeEscalation` - Pass Criteria: All namespaces have policies that restrict `allowPrivilegeEscalation` or the Pod Security Standards are set to "restricted" or "baseline". - Fail Criteria: Any namespace allows `allowPrivilegeEscalation: true` without a specific policy restricting it.

title: Minimize the admission of root containers
description: Do not generally permit containers to be run as the root user.
rationale: Containers may run as any Linux user. Containers which run as the root user, whilst constrained by Container Runtime security features still have an escalated likelihood of container breakout. Ideally, all containers should run as a defined non-UID 0 user. There should be at least one admission control policy defined which does not permit root containers. If you need to run root containers, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that each policy restricts the use of root containers by setting MustRunAsNonRoot or MustRunAs with the range of UIDs not including 0.
remediation: Create a policy for each namespace in the cluster, ensuring that either MustRunAsNonRoot or MustRunAs with the range of UIDs not including 0, is set.
impact: Pods with containers which run as the root user will not be permitted.
default_value: By default, there are no restrictions on the use of root containers and if a User is not specified in the image, the container will run as root.
justification: The compliance control can be effectively audited using Kubernetes APIs and kubectl commands. Although the first AI opinion suggested using Pod Security Policies (PSPs), which are deprecated, the second AI correctly pointed out the need to use the Pod Security Admission (PSA) controller, which is the current standard. This allows for an automated approach to check for policies that restrict the use of root containers.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pod Security Admission (PSA) - API/CLI Method: Use `kubectl` to interact with PSA configurations. - Properties to Check: Ensure that the PSA configuration for each namespace includes policies that enforce `MustRunAsNonRoot` or `MustRunAs` with UID ranges not including 0. - Pass Criteria: At least one PSA policy per namespace that restricts root containers as described. - Fail Criteria: No PSA policies or policies that allow root containers.

title: Minimize the admission of containers with the NET_RAW capability
description: Do not generally permit containers with the potentially dangerous NET_RAW capability.
rationale: Containers run with a default set of capabilities as assigned by the Container Runtime. By default this can include potentially dangerous capabilities. With Docker as the container runtime the NET_RAW capability is enabled which may be misused by malicious containers. Ideally, all containers should drop this capability. There should be at least one admission control policy defined which does not permit containers with the NET_RAW capability. If you need to run containers with this capability, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that at least one policy disallows the admission of containers with the NET_RAW capability.
remediation: Add policies to each namespace in the cluster which has user workloads to restrict the admission of containers with the NET_RAW capability.
impact: Pods with containers which run with the NET_RAW capability will not be permitted.
default_value: By default, there are no restrictions on the creation of containers with the NET_RAW capability.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the control can be assessed using Kubernetes API calls and `kubectl` commands. The second opinion correctly points out that Pod Security Policies (PSPs) are deprecated and suggests using modern alternatives like Open Policy Agent (OPA) Gatekeeper or Kyverno, which are suitable for enforcing such policies in current Kubernetes environments. This aligns with the control's requirement to ensure that policies disallow the NET_RAW capability.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Admission Controllers, specifically using OPA Gatekeeper or Kyverno. - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get constrainttemplates` and `kubectl get constraints` for OPA Gatekeeper; `kubectl get clusterpolicies` for Kyverno. - Properties to Check: Ensure that the constraint or policy explicitly disallows the NET_RAW capability in the `spec.rules` or equivalent field. - Pass Criteria: At least one constraint or policy exists that explicitly disallows the NET_RAW capability. - Fail Criteria: No constraints or policies exist that restrict the NET_RAW capability.

title: Minimize the admission of containers with added capabilities
description: Do not generally permit containers with capabilities assigned beyond the default set.
rationale: Containers run with a default set of capabilities as assigned by the Container Runtime. Capabilities outside this set can be added to containers which could expose them to risks of container breakout attacks. There should be at least one policy defined which prevents containers with capabilities beyond the default set from launching. If you need to run containers with additional capabilities, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that policies are present which prevent allowedCapabilities to be set to anything other than an empty array.
remediation: Ensure that allowedCapabilities is not present in policies for the cluster unless it is set to an empty array.
impact: Pods with containers which require capabilities with the default set will not be permitted.
default_value: By default, there are no restrictions on adding capabilities to containers.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the compliance control can be automated using Kubernetes APIs, specifically through the use of `kubectl` commands to check for policies that restrict container capabilities. Although Pod Security Policies (PSPs) are deprecated, alternative methods like Open Policy Agent (OPA) with Gatekeeper or Pod Security Admission (PSA) can be used for newer Kubernetes versions. The automation approach is technically feasible and aligns with the control's requirements to list and verify policies.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: PodSecurityPolicies (PSP) or equivalent policies like OPA/Gatekeeper, PSA - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get psp` or equivalent for OPA/PSA - Properties to Check: `allowedCapabilities` field in the policy specifications - Pass Criteria: `allowedCapabilities` is either absent or set to an empty array - Fail Criteria: `allowedCapabilities` is set to a non-empty array

title: Minimize the admission of containers with capabilities assigned
description: Do not generally permit containers with capabilities
rationale: Containers run with a default set of capabilities as assigned by the Container Runtime. Capabilities are parts of the rights generally granted on a Linux system to the root user. In many cases applications running in containers do not require any capabilities to operate, so from the perspective of the principal of least privilege use of capabilities should be minimized.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that at least one policy requires that capabilities are dropped by all containers.
remediation: Review the use of capabilities in applications running on your cluster. Where a namespace contains applications which do not require any Linux capabilities to operate consider adding a policy which forbids the admission of containers which do not drop all capabilities.
impact: Pods with containers require capabilities to operate will not be permitted.
default_value: By default, there are no restrictions on the creation of containers with additional capabilities
justification: The task of minimizing the admission of containers with capabilities assigned can be effectively automated using Kubernetes APIs and `kubectl` commands. Both AI opinions agree on the feasibility of automation, although the second opinion highlights the need to consider deprecated features like Pod Security Policies. The necessary checks can be performed by querying the Kubernetes API for Pod specifications and ensuring that the `securityContext.capabilities.drop` is set to `["ALL"]`. This approach leverages existing Kubernetes capabilities to enforce security policies consistently across the cluster.
automation_summary: - **Kubernetes Resource/Service:** Pods - **API/CLI Method:** `kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o jsonpath='{.items[*].spec.containers[*].securityContext.capabilities.drop}'` - **Properties to Check:** Ensure `securityContext.capabilities.drop` is set to `["ALL"]` for all containers. - **Pass Criteria:** All containers in all Pods have `securityContext.capabilities.drop` set to `["ALL"]`. - **Fail Criteria:** Any container in any Pod does not have `securityContext.capabilities.drop` set to `["ALL"]`.

title: Minimize the admission of Windows HostProcess Containers
description: Do not generally permit Windows containers to be run with the hostProcess flag set to true.
rationale: A Windows container making use of the hostProcess flag can interact with the underlying Windows cluster node. As per the Kubernetes documentation, this provides "privileged access" to the Windows node. Where Windows containers are used inside a Kubernetes cluster, there should be at least one admission control policy which does not permit hostProcess Windows containers. If you need to run Windows containers which require hostProcess , this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that each policy disallows the admission of hostProcess containers
remediation: Add policies to each namespace in the cluster which has user workloads to restrict the admission of hostProcess containers.
impact: Pods defined with securityContext.windowsOptions.hostProcess: true will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
default_value: By default, there are no restrictions on the creation of hostProcess containers.
justification: The control requires verifying that admission control policies disallow the use of the `hostProcess` flag in Windows containers. This can be efficiently automated using Kubernetes API calls and `kubectl` commands to list and inspect validating and mutating webhook configurations. Both AI opinions agree that this can be automated, although the second opinion suggests a need for more detailed rule verification, which can be incorporated into the automation process.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: ValidatingWebhookConfiguration, MutatingWebhookConfiguration - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get validatingwebhookconfiguration`, `kubectl get mutatingwebhookconfiguration` - Properties to Check: Ensure rules within these configurations explicitly deny `securityContext.windowsOptions.hostProcess: true` - Pass Criteria: Presence of admission control policies that explicitly restrict the use of `hostProcess` containers. - Fail Criteria: Absence of such policies or policies that allow `hostProcess` containers.

title: Minimize the admission of HostPath volumes
description: Do not generally admit containers which make use of hostPath volumes.
rationale: A container which mounts a hostPath volume as part of its specification will have access to the filesystem of the underlying cluster node. The use of hostPath volumes may allow containers access to privileged areas of the node filesystem. There should be at least one admission control policy defined which does not permit containers to mount hostPath volumes. If you need to run containers which require hostPath volumes, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that each policy disallows the admission of containers with hostPath volumes.
remediation: Add policies to each namespace in the cluster which has user workloads to restrict the admission of containers which use hostPath volumes.
impact: Pods defined which make use of hostPath volumes will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
default_value: By default, there are no restrictions on the creation of hostPath volumes.
justification: The compliance control requires verifying the presence of admission control policies that restrict the use of hostPath volumes. This can be effectively automated using Kubernetes APIs and `kubectl` commands to inspect the configurations of `ValidatingWebhookConfiguration` or `PodSecurityPolicy` (if applicable). Both AI opinions agree on the potential for automation, though the second opinion highlights the need for more precise command usage, which is achievable with Kubernetes capabilities.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Admission Controllers (e.g., ValidatingWebhookConfiguration, PodSecurityPolicy) - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get validatingwebhookconfigurations`, `kubectl get psp` - Properties to Check: Ensure that policies explicitly disallow `hostPath` volumes in their configurations. - Pass Criteria: Policies are present that explicitly disallow the use of `hostPath` volumes. - Fail Criteria: Absence of policies or policies that permit `hostPath` volumes.

title: Minimize the admission of containers which use HostPorts
description: Do not generally permit containers which require the use of HostPorts.
rationale: Host ports connect containers directly to the host's network. This can bypass controls such as network policy. There should be at least one admission control policy defined which does not permit containers which require the use of HostPorts. If you need to run containers which require HostPorts, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy.
audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that each policy disallows the admission of containers which have hostPort sections.
remediation: Add policies to each namespace in the cluster which has user workloads to restrict the admission of containers which use hostPort sections.
impact: Pods defined with hostPort settings in either the container, initContainer or ephemeralContainer sections will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
default_value: By default, there are no restrictions on the use of HostPorts.
justification: The compliance control can be automated by leveraging Kubernetes APIs to check for admission control policies that restrict the use of HostPorts. While the first AI opinion suggested using Pod Security Policies, which are deprecated, the second AI correctly pointed out the need to use newer mechanisms like Pod Security Admission or custom admission controllers. The Kubernetes API provides the necessary capabilities to automate this check by querying the current security configurations in place.
automation_summary: - **Kubernetes Resource/Service:** PodSecurityAdmission, Custom Admission Controllers - **API/CLI Method:** Use `kubectl` to query Pod Security Admission configurations or custom admission controllers. - **Properties to Check:** Ensure that the Pod Security Admission policies or custom admission controllers explicitly disallow `hostPort` usage. - **Pass Criteria:** At least one admission control policy exists that explicitly disallows `hostPort`. - **Fail Criteria:** No such policy exists, or existing policies allow `hostPort`.

title: Ensure that the CNI in use supports Network Policies
description: There are a variety of CNI plugins available for Kubernetes. If the CNI in use does not support Network Policies it may not be possible to effectively restrict traffic in the cluster.
rationale: Kubernetes network policies are enforced by the CNI plugin in use. As such it is important to ensure that the CNI plugin supports both Ingress and Egress network policies.
audit: Review the documentation of CNI plugin in use by the cluster, and confirm that it supports Ingress and Egress network policies.
remediation: If the CNI plugin in use does not support network policies, consideration should be given to making use of a different plugin, or finding an alternate mechanism for restricting traffic in the Kubernetes cluster.
default_value: This will depend on the CNI plugin in use.
additional_information: One example here is Flannel ( https://github.com/coreos/flannel ) which does not support Network policy unless Calico is also in use.
justification: The task of verifying whether a CNI plugin supports Network Policies involves checking the plugin's documentation, which is not something that can be fully automated through Kubernetes APIs or CLI commands. While certain aspects, such as identifying the CNI plugin in use, can be automated, the critical step of confirming support for Network Policies requires manual review of the plugin's documentation or vendor-specific resources. This step is essential and cannot be bypassed by automation.
manual_summary: 1. Identify the CNI plugin in use by executing `kubectl get pods -n kube-system` and look for pods related to common CNI plugins like Calico, Cilium, or Weave Net. 2. Access the CNI configuration files on the nodes, typically located at `/etc/cni/net.d/`, by using `kubectl exec` to enter a node and view the configuration. 3. Review the official documentation of the identified CNI plugin to verify that it explicitly supports both Ingress and Egress network policies. 4. Confirm compliance by ensuring that the documentation states support for Network Policies and that the configuration aligns with the documented capabilities.

title: Ensure that all Namespaces have Network Policies defined
description: Use network policies to isolate traffic in your cluster network.
rationale: Running different applications on the same Kubernetes cluster creates a risk of one compromised application attacking a neighboring application. Network segmentation is important to ensure that containers can communicate only with those they are supposed to. A network policy is a specification of how selections of pods are allowed to communicate with each other and other network endpoints. Network Policies are namespace scoped. When a network policy is introduced to a given namespace, all traffic not allowed by the policy is denied. However, if there are no network policies in a namespace all traffic will be allowed into and out of the pods in that namespace.
audit: Run the below command and review the NetworkPolicy objects created in the cluster. kubectl --all-namespaces get networkpolicy Ensure that each namespace defined in the cluster has at least one Network Policy.
remediation: Follow the documentation and create NetworkPolicy objects as you need them.
impact: Once network policies are in use within a given namespace, traffic not explicitly allowed by a network policy will be denied. As such it is important to ensure that, when introducing network policies, legitimate traffic is not blocked.
default_value: By default, network policies are not created.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the audit can be automated using Kubernetes APIs, specifically through the `kubectl` command to check for Network Policies across all namespaces. The second opinion suggests an improvement by ensuring that all namespaces are checked for the presence of at least one Network Policy, which can be done by listing all namespaces and cross-referencing them with those that have Network Policies. This task is feasible with Kubernetes CLI capabilities.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: NetworkPolicies - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get namespaces` and `kubectl get networkpolicy --all-namespaces` - Properties to Check: Ensure each namespace has at least one NetworkPolicy defined. - Pass Criteria: Every namespace listed by `kubectl get namespaces` has at least one corresponding NetworkPolicy. - Fail Criteria: At least one namespace does not have any NetworkPolicy defined.

title: Prefer using secrets as files over secrets as environment variables
description: Kubernetes supports mounting secrets as data volumes or as environment variables. Minimize the use of environment variable secrets.
rationale: It is reasonably common for application code to log out its environment (particularly in the event of an error). This will include any secret values passed in as environment variables, so secrets can easily be exposed to any user or entity who has access to the logs.
audit: Run the following command to find references to objects which use environment variables defined from secrets. kubectl get all -o jsonpath='{range .items[?(@..secretKeyRef)]} {.kind} {.metadata.name} {"\n"}{end}' -A
remediation: If possible, rewrite application code to read secrets from mounted secret files, rather than from environment variables.
impact: Application code which expects to read secrets in the form of environment variables would need modification
default_value: By default, secrets are not defined
additional_information: Mounting secrets as volumes has the additional benefit that secret values can be updated without restarting the pod
justification: The compliance control can be effectively audited using Kubernetes APIs through the `kubectl` command, which allows querying for resources that use secrets as environment variables. Both AI opinions agree on the feasibility of automation, and the command provided is technically sound for identifying non-compliant resources. The use of `kubectl` is appropriate for this task as it can access the necessary configuration details across all namespaces.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: All resources (e.g., Pods, Deployments, StatefulSets) - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get all -o jsonpath='{range .items[?(@..secretKeyRef)]} {.kind} {.metadata.name} {"\n"}{end}' -A` - Properties to Check: Presence of `secretKeyRef` in environment variables - Pass Criteria: No output, indicating no resources are using secrets as environment variables - Fail Criteria: Output listing resources, indicating non-compliance

title: Consider external secret storage
description: Consider the use of an external secrets storage and management system, instead of using Kubernetes Secrets directly, if you have more complex secret management needs. Ensure the solution requires authentication to access secrets, has auditing of access to and use of secrets, and encrypts secrets. Some solutions also make it easier to rotate secrets.
rationale: Kubernetes supports secrets as first-class objects, but care needs to be taken to ensure that access to secrets is carefully limited. Using an external secrets provider can ease the management of access to secrets, especially where secrests are used across both Kubernetes and non-Kubernetes environments.
audit: Review your secrets management implementation.
remediation: Refer to the secrets management options offered by your cloud provider or a third-party secrets management solution.
default_value: By default, no external secret management is configured.
justification: While some aspects of secret management can be automated, such as identifying the use of Kubernetes Secrets with `kubectl`, the comprehensive evaluation of external secret management solutions requires manual inspection. This includes verifying authentication mechanisms, auditing capabilities, and encryption settings, which are typically configured outside of Kubernetes and require reviewing external systems' documentation and configurations.
manual_summary: 1. Review the current secrets management implementation in your Kubernetes environment by checking the configurations of your applications and how they access secrets. 2. Verify if an external secrets management solution is in use by looking for integrations with tools like HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, or Azure Key Vault. 3. Confirm that the external secrets management solution requires authentication to access secrets by reviewing the documentation of the secrets management tool. 4. Check if the solution provides auditing capabilities for access to and use of secrets by reviewing logs or audit trails provided by the secrets management tool. 5. Ensure that secrets are encrypted both at rest and in transit by checking the configuration settings of the external secrets management solution. 6. Document your findings and ensure that any gaps in the secrets management process are addressed, potentially by implementing a recommended solution if none is in place.

title: Configure Image Provenance using ImagePolicyWebhook admission controller
description: Configure Image Provenance for your deployment.
rationale: Kubernetes supports plugging in provenance rules to accept or reject the images in your deployments. You could configure such rules to ensure that only approved images are deployed in the cluster.
audit: Review the pod definitions in your cluster and verify that image provenance is configured as appropriate.
remediation: Follow the Kubernetes documentation and setup image provenance.
impact: You need to regularly maintain your provenance configuration based on container image updates.
default_value: By default, image provenance is not set.
justification: The configuration of the ImagePolicyWebhook admission controller can be verified through automated checks using Kubernetes API and CLI commands. Both AI opinions agree that this is primarily an automated task, though the second opinion suggests refining the command for accuracy. The presence of the ImagePolicyWebhook can be programmatically verified by checking the API server's configuration, which is feasible with Kubernetes capabilities.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: API Server - API/CLI Method: Use `kubectl` to inspect the API server's configuration, specifically looking for the `--enable-admission-plugins` flag. - Properties to Check: Ensure that `ImagePolicyWebhook` is listed under the `--enable-admission-plugins` flag. - Pass Criteria: The `--enable-admission-plugins` flag includes `ImagePolicyWebhook`. - Fail Criteria: The `--enable-admission-plugins` flag does not include `ImagePolicyWebhook`.

title: Create administrative boundaries between resources using namespaces
description: Use namespaces to isolate your Kubernetes objects.
rationale: Limiting the scope of user permissions can reduce the impact of mistakes or malicious activities. A Kubernetes namespace allows you to partition created resources into logically named groups. Resources created in one namespace can be hidden from other namespaces. By default, each resource created by a user in Kubernetes cluster runs in a default namespace, called default . You can create additional namespaces and attach resources and users to them. You can use Kubernetes Authorization plugins to create policies that segregate access to namespace resources between different users.
audit: Run the below command and review the namespaces created in the cluster. kubectl get namespaces Ensure that these namespaces are the ones you need and are adequately administered as per your requirements.
remediation: Follow the documentation and create namespaces for objects in your deployment as you need them.
impact: You need to switch between namespaces for administration.
default_value: By default, Kubernetes starts with 4 initial namespaces: 1. default - The default namespace for objects with no other namespace 2. kube-system - The namespace for objects created by the Kubernetes system 3. kube-node-lease - Namespace used for node heartbeats 4. kube-public - Namespace used for public information in a cluster Internal Only - General
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the audit can be automated using Kubernetes CLI commands, specifically `kubectl get namespaces`. This command allows for the listing and verification of namespaces, which is a core feature of Kubernetes and can be effectively automated. The second opinion suggests additional checks for namespace configurations like resource quotas and network policies, which can also be automated using Kubernetes APIs.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Namespaces - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get namespaces`, `kubectl get resourcequotas --namespace=<namespace>`, `kubectl get networkpolicies --namespace=<namespace>` - Properties to Check: Presence of required namespaces, resource quotas, and network policies for each namespace - Pass Criteria: All required namespaces are present, and each namespace has appropriate resource quotas and network policies configured. - Fail Criteria: Missing required namespaces, or namespaces without proper resource quotas and network policies.

title: Ensure that the seccomp profile is set to docker/default in your pod definitions
description: Enable docker/default seccomp profile in your pod definitions.
rationale: Seccomp (secure computing mode) is used to restrict the set of system calls applications can make, allowing cluster administrators greater control over the security of workloads running in the cluster. Kubernetes disables seccomp profiles by default for historical reasons. You should enable it to ensure that the workloads have restricted actions available within the container.
audit: Review the pod definitions in your cluster. It should create a line as below: securityContext: seccompProfile: type: RuntimeDefault
remediation: Use security context to enable the docker/default seccomp profile in your pod definitions. An example is as below: securityContext: seccompProfile: type: RuntimeDefault
impact: If the docker/default seccomp profile is too restrictive for you, you would have to create/manage your own seccomp profiles.
default_value: By default, seccomp profile is set to unconfined which means that no seccomp profiles are enabled.
justification: The compliance control can be effectively audited using Kubernetes APIs and CLI tools. Both AI opinions agree that an automated approach is feasible, though the second opinion highlights the need for a more comprehensive command that checks both Pod-level and container-level `securityContext` settings. This ensures that the seccomp profile is correctly set to `RuntimeDefault` across all relevant levels.
automation_summary: - **Kubernetes Resource/Service:** Pods - **API/CLI Method:** Use `kubectl` with `jsonpath` to extract security context information. - **Properties to Check:** `.spec.securityContext.seccompProfile.type` at both Pod and container levels. - **Pass Criteria:** All Pods and their containers have `securityContext.seccompProfile.type` set to `RuntimeDefault`. - **Fail Criteria:** Any Pod or container does not have `securityContext.seccompProfile.type` set to `RuntimeDefault`.

title: Apply Security Context to Your Pods and Containers
description: Apply Security Context to Your Pods and Containers
rationale: A security context defines the operating system security settings (uid, gid, capabilities, SELinux role, etc..) applied to a container. When designing your containers and pods, make sure that you configure the security context for your pods, containers, and volumes. A security context is a property defined in the deployment yaml. It controls the security parameters that will be assigned to the pod/container/volume. There are two levels of security context: pod level security context, and container level security context.
audit: Review the pod definitions in your cluster and verify that you have security contexts defined as appropriate.
remediation: Follow the Kubernetes documentation and apply security contexts to your pods. For a suggested list of security contexts, you may refer to the CIS Security Benchmark for Docker Containers.
impact: If you incorrectly apply security contexts, you may have trouble running the pods.
default_value: By default, no security contexts are automatically applied to pods.
justification: The control requires checking the application of security contexts to pods and containers, which can be efficiently verified using Kubernetes APIs and `kubectl` commands. Both AI opinions agree that the presence of security contexts can be programmatically checked, and the necessary fields are accessible via the Kubernetes API. While manual interpretation might be needed for specific policy compliance, the primary audit task of verifying the presence of security contexts is well-suited for automation.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o json` - Properties to Check: `spec.containers[*].securityContext`, `spec.securityContext` - Pass Criteria: Security contexts are defined with appropriate settings such as `runAsUser`, `runAsGroup`, `privileged`, and `capabilities`. - Fail Criteria: Absence of security contexts or configurations that do not align with security best practices (e.g., running as root, excessive privileges).

title: The default namespace should not be used
description: Kubernetes provides a default namespace, where objects are placed if no namespace is specified for them. Placing objects in this namespace makes application of RBAC and other controls more difficult.
rationale: Resources in a Kubernetes cluster should be segregated by namespace, to allow for security controls to be applied at that level and to make it easier to manage resources.
audit: Run this command to list objects in default namespace kubectl get $(kubectl api-resources --verbs=list --namespaced=true -o name | paste -sd, -) --ignore-not-found -n default The only entries there should be system managed resources such as the kubernetes service
remediation: Ensure that namespaces are created to allow for appropriate segregation of Kubernetes resources and that all new resources are created in a specific namespace.
default_value: Unless a namespace is specific on object creation, the default namespace will be used
justification: Both AI opinions agree that this compliance control can be effectively audited using Kubernetes CLI tools, specifically `kubectl`. The command provided in the audit steps is capable of listing all resources in the default namespace, which aligns with the control's requirement to ensure that only system-managed resources are present. This task is well-suited for automation as it leverages the existing Kubernetes APIs and CLI capabilities.
automation_summary: - **Kubernetes Resource/Service:** Namespaced resources (e.g., Pods, Services, Deployments) - **API/CLI Method:** `kubectl get $(kubectl api-resources --verbs=list --namespaced=true -o name | paste -sd, -) --ignore-not-found -n default` - **Properties to Check:** Presence of user-created resources in the default namespace - **Pass Criteria:** The output shows only system-managed resources (e.g., the Kubernetes service) - **Fail Criteria:** The output includes any user-created resources in the default namespace

title: Ensure that the API Server only makes use of Strong Cryptographic Ciphers
description: Ensure that the API server is configured to only use strong cryptographic ciphers.
rationale: TLS ciphers have had a number of known vulnerabilities and weaknesses, which can reduce the protection provided by them. By default Kubernetes supports a number of TLS ciphersuites including some that have security concerns, weakening the protection provided.
audit: Run the following command on the Control Plane node: ps -ef | grep kube-apiserver Verify that the --tls-cipher-suites argument is set as outlined in the remediation procedure below.
remediation: Edit the API server pod specification file /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml on the Control Plane node and set the below parameter. --tls-cipher-suites=TLS_AES_128_GCM_SHA256, TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384, TLS_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256, TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA, TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256, TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_256_CBC_SHA, TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384, TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305, TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256, TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_3DES_EDE_CBC_SHA, TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA, TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256, TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_CBC_SHA, TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384, TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305, TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256, TLS_RSA_WITH_3DES_EDE_CBC_SHA, TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA, TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256, TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_256_CBC_SHA, TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384.
impact: API server clients that cannot support modern cryptographic ciphers will not be able to make connections to the API server.
default_value: By default the Kubernetes API server supports a wide range of TLS ciphers
additional_information: The list chosen above should be fine for modern clients. It's essentially the list from the Mozilla "Modern cipher" option with the ciphersuites supporting CBC mode removed, as CBC has traditionally had a lot of issues
justification: The compliance control can be effectively audited using Kubernetes APIs or `kubectl` to verify the API server's command-line arguments, specifically the `--tls-cipher-suites` parameter. Both AI opinions agree on the feasibility of automation, and the necessary information is accessible through Kubernetes commands, making manual inspection unnecessary.
automation_summary: - **Kubernetes Resource/Service:** Pods (specifically the API server pod) - **API/CLI Method:** `kubectl get pod -n kube-system -l component=kube-apiserver -o jsonpath='{.items[*].spec.containers[*].command}'` - **Properties to Check:** Presence of the `--tls-cipher-suites` argument with the specified strong cipher suites. - **Pass Criteria:** The command output includes `--tls-cipher-suites` followed by the specified strong ciphers: `TLS_AES_128_GCM_SHA256`, `TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384`, `TLS_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256`, etc. - **Fail Criteria:** Absence of the `--tls-cipher-suites` argument or inclusion of weak or unspecified ciphers.

title: Ensure that the --encryption-provider-config argument is set as appropriate
description: Encrypt etcd key-value store.
rationale: etcd is a highly available key-value store used by Kubernetes deployments for persistent storage of all of its REST API objects. These objects are sensitive in nature and should be encrypted at rest to avoid any disclosures.
audit: Run the following command on the master node: ps -ef | grep kube-apiserver Verify that the --encryption-provider-config argument is set to a EncryptionConfig file. Additionally, ensure that the EncryptionConfig file has all the desired resources covered especially any secrets.
remediation: Follow the Kubernetes documentation and configure a EncryptionConfig file. Then, edit the API server pod specification file /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml on the master node and set the --encryption-provider-config parameter to the path of that file: --encryption-provider-config=</path/to/EncryptionConfig/File>
default_value: By default, --encryption-provider-config is not set.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the compliance control can be automated by verifying the presence of the `--encryption-provider-config` argument using Kubernetes CLI commands. The kube-apiserver's configuration can be accessed programmatically via `kubectl`, making it feasible to automate this check. The ability to query the kube-apiserver's arguments directly aligns with Kubernetes API capabilities, confirming that automation is technically possible.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l component=kube-apiserver -o jsonpath='{.items[*].spec.containers[*].args}'` - Properties to Check: Presence of `--encryption-provider-config=<path/to/EncryptionConfig/File>` - Pass Criteria: Output contains `--encryption-provider-config` followed by a valid path to the EncryptionConfig file. - Fail Criteria: Absence of the `--encryption-provider-config` argument or an invalid path indicates non-compliance.

title: Create administrative boundaries between resources using namespaces
description: Use namespaces to isolate your Kubernetes objects.
rationale: Limiting the scope of user permissions can reduce the impact of mistakes or malicious activities. A Kubernetes namespace allows you to partition created resources into logically named groups. Resources created in one namespace can be hidden from other namespaces. By default, each resource created by a user in Kubernetes cluster runs in a default namespace, called default . You can create additional namespaces and attach resources and users to them. You can use Kubernetes Authorization plugins to create policies that segregate access to namespace resources between different users.
audit: Run the below command and review the namespaces created in the cluster. kubectl get namespaces Ensure that these namespaces are the ones you need and are adequately administered as per your requirements.
remediation: Follow the documentation and create namespaces for objects in your deployment as you need them.
impact: You need to switch between namespaces for administration.
default_value: By default, Kubernetes starts with two initial namespaces: 1. default - The default namespace for objects with no other namespace 2. kube-system - The namespace for objects created by the Kubernetes system 3. kube-node-lease - Namespace used for node heartbeats 4. kube-public - Namespace used for public information in a cluster
justification: The task of verifying namespace creation and administration can be effectively automated using Kubernetes CLI commands. The command `kubectl get namespaces` provides a straightforward method to list all namespaces, which aligns with the compliance control's audit steps. While the second opinion suggests additional checks for network policies and resource quotas, the primary requirement is to ensure namespaces are created and managed, which is achievable through automation.
automation_summary: - **Kubernetes Resource/Service:** Namespaces - **API/CLI Method:** `kubectl get namespaces` - **Properties to Check:** List of namespaces returned by the command - **Pass Criteria:** The output should include all expected namespaces as per organizational requirements, such as specific namespaces for different teams or applications. - **Fail Criteria:** Missing expected namespaces or presence of unauthorized namespaces indicates non-compliance.

title: Ensure that the seccomp profile is set to docker/default in your pod definitions
description: Enable docker/default seccomp profile in your pod definitions.
rationale: Seccomp (secure computing mode) is used to restrict the set of system calls applications can make, allowing cluster administrators greater control over the security of workloads running in the cluster. Kubernetes disables seccomp profiles by default for historical reasons. You should enable it to ensure that the workloads have restricted actions available within the container.
audit: Review the pod definitions in your cluster. It should create a line as below: securityContext: seccompProfile: type: RuntimeDefault
remediation: Use security context to enable the docker/default seccomp profile in your pod definitions. An example is as below: securityContext: seccompProfile: type: RuntimeDefault
impact: If the docker/default seccomp profile is too restrictive for you, you would have to create/manage your own seccomp profiles.
default_value: By default, seccomp profile is set to unconfined which means that no seccomp profiles are enabled.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the audit of the seccomp profile can be automated using Kubernetes APIs and `kubectl` commands. The seccomp profile is a configuration that can be retrieved from pod specifications, making it feasible to automate the compliance check. The second opinion correctly points out the need to check both pod-level and container-level configurations, which can be addressed with an enhanced command.
automation_summary: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o jsonpath` - Properties to Check: `.spec.securityContext.seccompProfile.type` and `.spec.containers[*].securityContext.seccompProfile.type` - Pass Criteria: All pods and their containers have `seccompProfile.type` set to `RuntimeDefault`. - Fail Criteria: Any pod or container has `seccompProfile.type` set to `Unconfined` or is missing the seccomp profile configuration.

title: Apply Security Context to Your Pods and Containers
description: Apply Security Context to Your Pods and Containers
rationale: A security context defines the operating system security settings (uid, gid, capabilities, SELinux role, etc..) applied to a container. When designing your containers and pods, make sure that you configure the security context for your pods, containers, and volumes. A security context is a property defined in the deployment yaml. It controls the security parameters that will be assigned to the pod/container/volume. There are two levels of security context: pod level security context, and container level security context.
audit: Review the pod definitions in your cluster and verify that you have security contexts defined as appropriate.
remediation: Follow the Kubernetes documentation and apply security contexts to your pods. For a suggested list of security contexts, you may refer to the CIS Security Benchmark for Docker Containers.
impact: If you incorrectly apply security contexts, you may have trouble running the pods.
default_value: By default, no security contexts are automatically applied to pods.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that the audit of security contexts in pod specifications can be automated using Kubernetes APIs or the `kubectl` command-line tool. The task involves checking the presence and correctness of security context fields, which are accessible programmatically. Kubernetes provides the necessary capabilities to automate this compliance check effectively, making manual inspection unnecessary.
